title: db_018_explain_composite_index.sql
descriptionMd:
## Mission: The Query Has An Index — Why Is It Still A Seq Scan?

A dashboard query filters orders by `(customer_id, status, placed_at)` and is taking 12 seconds against a 50M-row table. There IS an index — `CREATE INDEX idx_orders_status ON orders (status);` — but `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` shows a sequential scan.

```
Seq Scan on orders  (cost=0.00..1024381.00 rows=842 width=128)
                    (actual time=4231.118..11982.501 rows=812 loops=1)
  Filter: ((customer_id = 5042)
           AND (status = 'shipped')
           AND (placed_at >= '2026-01-01'))
  Rows Removed by Filter: 49999188
```

The `status` index is *too unselective* — `status = 'shipped'` matches ~30% of all orders, so the planner correctly decided a seq scan was cheaper than reading 15M index entries plus the heap. The right move is a **composite index** on the columns the query actually filters by, in the order that maximises selectivity.
initialCode:
-- Existing index — single column, not selective enough.
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_status ON orders (status);

-- Query the dashboard runs.
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT id, order_total, placed_at
FROM   orders
WHERE  customer_id = 5042
  AND  status      = 'shipped'
  AND  placed_at  >= '2026-01-01';
traceback:
QUERY PLAN
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Seq Scan on orders  (cost=0.00..1024381.00 rows=842 width=128)
                     (actual time=4231.118..11982.501 rows=812 loops=1)
   Filter: ((customer_id = 5042)
            AND (status = 'shipped')
            AND (placed_at >= '2026-01-01'))
   Rows Removed by Filter: 49999188
 Planning Time: 0.412 ms
 Execution Time: 11982.671 ms

50M rows scanned; ~812 returned. The status index exists but is too unselective
for the planner to prefer it (status='shipped' matches ~30% of rows). 12 seconds.
correctOutput:
QUERY PLAN
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Index Scan using idx_orders_customer_status_placedat on orders
   (cost=0.56..82.34 rows=812 width=128)
   (actual time=0.082..1.341 rows=812 loops=1)
   Index Cond: ((customer_id = 5042)
                AND (status = 'shipped')
                AND (placed_at >= '2026-01-01'))
 Planning Time: 0.187 ms
 Execution Time: 1.521 ms

Index scan; ~812 rows fetched directly. 12 seconds → 1.5 ms.
8000× faster on the same data.

options.label: Create a composite index on (customer_id, status, placed_at) — most-selective column first
options.patchCode:
-- Drop the unselective single-column index (planner won't use it for this query anyway).
DROP INDEX IF EXISTS idx_orders_status;

-- Create a composite index. Order matters:
--   1. customer_id — highly selective (one customer out of millions).
--   2. status      — moderately selective.
--   3. placed_at   — range column, last so the index can be used for the range scan.
--
-- Rule of thumb: equality columns first (in order of selectivity), range columns last.
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_customer_status_placedat
  ON orders (customer_id, status, placed_at);

-- Update planner statistics so the new index is considered immediately.
ANALYZE orders;

EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT id, order_total, placed_at
FROM   orders
WHERE  customer_id = 5042
  AND  status      = 'shipped'
  AND  placed_at  >= '2026-01-01';
options.resultLog:
QUERY PLAN
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Index Scan using idx_orders_customer_status_placedat on orders
   (cost=0.56..82.34 rows=812 width=128)
   (actual time=0.082..1.341 rows=812 loops=1)
   Index Cond: ((customer_id = 5042) AND (status = 'shipped') AND (placed_at >= '2026-01-01'))
 Planning Time: 0.187 ms
 Execution Time: 1.521 ms

50M rows scanned → ~812 fetched directly. 12,000 ms → 1.5 ms. 8000× faster.
options.rationale: A **composite (multi-column) index** is a single B-tree whose key is the concatenation of the column values, in the declared order. For the WHERE clause `customer_id = X AND status = Y AND placed_at >= Z`, the planner can traverse the index directly to the `(X, Y, Z..)` region and scan just the matching rows. The column order in the declaration matters: equality columns first (most-selective first, so the prefix narrows the candidates quickly), then range columns last (the range scan can only use the trailing portion of the index once equality is fixed). `ANALYZE orders` updates the planner's statistics so it knows to use the new index immediately rather than relying on the next autovacuum cycle. The throughput gain is dramatic — the planner now reads ~812 index entries plus their heap rows instead of scanning 50M rows.

options.label: Create three separate single-column indexes — one on each filtered column
options.resultLog:
QUERY PLAN
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Bitmap Heap Scan on orders  (cost=42.81..1247.62 rows=812 width=128)
                              (actual time=18.221..184.503 rows=812 loops=1)
   Recheck Cond: (customer_id = 5042)
   Filter: ((status = 'shipped') AND (placed_at >= '2026-01-01'))
   ->  Bitmap Index Scan on idx_orders_customer_id  (cost=0.00..42.61 rows=1893 width=0)
         Index Cond: (customer_id = 5042)
 Execution Time: 184.5 ms

12 sec → 0.18 sec. Better, but 100× slower than the composite index because:
  - Planner picks the most selective single-column index (customer_id, ~1893 rows).
  - Reads those 1893 rows from the heap.
  - Filters in memory for status='shipped' AND placed_at >= '2026-01-01'.
  - Other two indexes are unused for this query but cost storage and slow every INSERT.

Three single-column indexes is the 'naive' answer; one composite index is the right one.
options.rationale: Three single-column indexes IS better than one bad one — the planner picks the most selective (`customer_id`) and uses it to find ~1893 candidate rows, then filters those in memory for the remaining conditions. The 184 ms is a real improvement over 12,000 ms but still 100× slower than the composite index, which can use ALL three predicates as the index condition. Other costs: three indexes mean three B-trees to maintain on every INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE (writes are 3× more expensive per affected column), and three indexes worth of storage on a 50M-row table. The composite index does the same job with one B-tree, faster reads, and a single write-amplification cost. This is the canonical 'know when to combine your indexes' lesson.

options.label: Add an index on (status, customer_id, placed_at) — same columns, status first
options.resultLog:
QUERY PLAN
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Index Scan using idx_orders_status_customer_placedat on orders
   (cost=0.56..18234.00 rows=812 width=128)
   (actual time=18.221..421.503 rows=812 loops=1)
   Index Cond: ((status = 'shipped') AND (customer_id = 5042) AND (placed_at >= '2026-01-01'))
 Execution Time: 421.5 ms

0.4 sec — usable, but 300× slower than (customer_id, status, placed_at).

Why: the index is keyed first on status, which matches ~30% of all rows.
The planner must walk 15M index entries that match status='shipped' until it reaches
the (5042, ...) section. With customer_id first, the prefix narrows the candidates
to ~1893 entries immediately, then status and placed_at refine within that tiny subset.
options.rationale: Column order in a composite index matters — **a lot**. The B-tree is keyed on the concatenation of values in the declared order, so the index is most useful when the leading column has high selectivity (narrows the candidate set quickly). `status` with three values ('pending', 'shipped', 'cancelled') is low-selectivity; the prefix `status='shipped'` still contains ~15M entries, and the planner has to walk a large portion of the index to find the `(shipped, 5042, ...)` region. With `customer_id` first, the prefix `customer_id=5042` immediately narrows to ~1893 entries, then `status` and `placed_at` refine within that tiny subset. **Rule of thumb**: equality columns first, in order of selectivity (most selective first), then range columns last.

hints: What does the `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` output say the planner expected vs got? `status = 'shipped'` matches ~30% of all rows — too high for the planner to prefer the single-column status index. What single index could the planner use to satisfy all three predicates at once?
Create a composite index `(customer_id, status, placed_at)` — equality columns first in order of selectivity, range column last. `ANALYZE orders` afterwards so the planner sees the new statistics.
explanationMd:
### Why the bug occurs
The planner chose a sequential scan because the existing index (`status`) was too unselective to help. With `status = 'shipped'` matching ~30% of rows, using the index would require reading ~15M index entries plus the heap rows they reference — more I/O than just scanning the table sequentially. The planner's cost-based decision was correct given the indexes available; the available indexes were the bug.

### Why the fix is correct
A **composite (multi-column) index** is a single B-tree keyed on the concatenation of column values in the declared order. The query's WHERE clause matches the index's column order exactly, so the planner can do an **index scan** that consumes only the matching prefix region — about 812 entries instead of 50M. Heap fetches follow only for the matched rows.

### Composite index column order — the rule
Equality columns first (most selective first), range columns last. For the query:
```sql
WHERE customer_id = 5042 AND status = 'shipped' AND placed_at >= '2026-01-01'
```
- `customer_id` — equality, very selective (~1893 of 50M rows = 0.004%).
- `status` — equality, moderately selective.
- `placed_at` — range, last so it can use the trailing portion of the index.

With this order, the index efficiently narrows from 50M → 1893 → ~812. With the columns in a different order (e.g. `status, customer_id, placed_at`), the leading prefix is too wide; the index still helps but not as much.

### Composite index vs multiple single-column indexes
Three single-column indexes — `(customer_id)`, `(status)`, `(placed_at)` — let the planner pick the most selective (`customer_id`) and bitmap-scan with it, then filter the remaining conditions in memory. This works but is slower than the composite index (which uses all three predicates as the index condition) and triples the write cost and storage.

When to prefer single-column indexes: when each column is queried independently by different queries. When to prefer composite: when several columns are queried together in a known pattern (the dashboard, the analytics query, the report).

### Partial indexes — when most rows don't matter
If the dashboard only ever queries `status = 'shipped'`, a **partial index** is even smaller:
```sql
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_shipped_customer_placedat
  ON orders (customer_id, placed_at)
  WHERE status = 'shipped';
```
Only shipped orders are indexed; the index is ~30% the size of a full index, faster to maintain, and the planner uses it for any query that includes `WHERE status = 'shipped'`.

### Always ANALYZE after creating an index
Planner statistics are kept up to date by autovacuum, but autovacuum runs on a schedule, not immediately. After creating a new index — especially one you expect a hot query to use right away — run `ANALYZE <table>` so the planner has fresh statistics and starts using the new index in the next plan.

### Read the plan, don't guess
`EXPLAIN` shows the planner's estimate; `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` runs the query and shows actual measured times. When estimates and actuals diverge dramatically, the statistics are stale (re-run `ANALYZE`) or the planner has incomplete information (consider `EXTENDED STATISTICS`). The cheapest debugging tool you have is `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` — read it before changing indexes.
recruiterReview: Correct fix and you named the right principle (composite-index column order based on selectivity). The three-single-column-indexes distractor is the most common naive answer; it works, but it's expensive to maintain and slower at query time than one composite. The reversed-column-order distractor is the subtler trap — engineers know they need a composite but pick the order based on column names rather than selectivity. One follow-up for production: when you have multiple hot queries with different column orders, you sometimes end up with redundant composite indexes (`(a, b, c)` and `(a, c, b)`) — that's expected; the alternative is making one query slow to save the other's index. The art is knowing which queries actually need the optimisation. Another technique worth knowing: `INCLUDE` columns in PostgreSQL 11+ — extra columns stored in the index leaf pages without being part of the key, useful for index-only scans. Approved. ✅

tutorial.bodyMd:
## Composite Indexes and Selectivity

When a query filters on multiple columns, a single composite index is usually faster than multiple single-column indexes. Choosing the right composite — and the right column order within it — is one of the highest-leverage performance optimisations in any database-heavy app.

### What a composite index is
A composite index is a single B-tree whose key is the concatenation of column values in the declared order. For `CREATE INDEX idx ON t (a, b, c)`, the index is sorted first by `a`, then by `b` within each `a`, then by `c` within each `(a, b)`.

Queries that filter on a **prefix** of the index columns can use it:
- `WHERE a = ?` — uses the index.
- `WHERE a = ? AND b = ?` — uses the index.
- `WHERE a = ? AND b = ? AND c = ?` — uses the index.
- `WHERE a = ? AND c = ?` — uses the index for the `a = ?` predicate only; filters on `c` in memory.
- `WHERE b = ?` — index NOT useful (no leading prefix).
- `WHERE c = ?` — index NOT useful.

This is the **leftmost prefix rule**. It governs every composite-index decision.

### Column order: equality first, range last
The rule of thumb for composite-index column order:
1. **Equality predicates first**, in order of selectivity (most selective first).
2. **Range predicates last** (`>`, `<`, `BETWEEN`).
3. **`ORDER BY` columns** can be placed after the equality columns to support sort-free retrieval.

Why: a B-tree can efficiently restrict to the matching prefix range using equality conditions. Once it hits a range condition, the remaining columns can't be used for further index restriction — they're just stored sorted within the matched range.

### Selectivity matters
**Selectivity** is the fraction of rows that match a given predicate. Highly selective predicates (`customer_id = 5042` matching 0.004% of rows) narrow the candidate set drastically; low-selectivity predicates (`status = 'shipped'` matching 30%) don't narrow much.

For the leading column of a composite index, pick the most selective. The leading column does most of the work; the trailing columns refine.

### Composite vs multiple single-column indexes
- **Composite** wins for queries that filter on multiple columns in a known order, especially when those columns are frequently used together.
- **Multiple single-column** wins for queries that filter on each column independently in different queries.

The write cost matters too: every index has to be updated on every INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE of the indexed row. Three indexes = 3× write amplification; one composite = 1× even if it covers three columns.

### Partial indexes
When most rows don't match a hot predicate, a **partial index** is even smaller:
```sql
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_pending
  ON orders (created_at)
  WHERE status = 'pending';
```
Only pending orders are indexed; the index is ~10% the size of a full index. The planner uses it for any query that includes `WHERE status = 'pending'`. Great for queue-style tables where only the unprocessed rows are queried frequently.

### Covering indexes and INCLUDE columns
A **covering index** contains all the columns a query needs, so the planner can answer the query from the index alone without fetching from the heap. PostgreSQL 11+ supports non-key columns via `INCLUDE`:
```sql
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_lookup
  ON orders (customer_id, status)
  INCLUDE (order_total, placed_at);
```
The leaf pages store `order_total` and `placed_at` for free — included in the index without being part of the key. Index-only scans work for queries that read only the indexed and included columns.

### Reading EXPLAIN ANALYZE
The cheapest debugging tool you have. Run it before changing indexes:
```sql
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT TEXT)
SELECT ... FROM ... WHERE ...;
```
Look for:
- **Seq Scan** on a large table with a small `Rows Returned` — missing index.
- **`(col)::cast`** in the Filter — function defeats index; build an expression index.
- **Bitmap Heap Scan** with many `Rows Removed by Filter` — index too unselective; consider composite.
- **Index Scan** with high `actual time` relative to `cost` — stale statistics; `ANALYZE`.
- **`Buffers: shared read=...`** much higher than `hit` — cold cache; query is doing disk I/O.

### Don't index everything
Every index slows writes and uses storage. The rule:
- **Index what you query often** — hot path columns in `WHERE`, `JOIN ON`, `ORDER BY`.
- **Don't index what you don't query** — columns that only appear in `SELECT` lists are not index candidates.
- **Drop unused indexes** — `pg_stat_user_indexes.idx_scan = 0` means an index that's never used; it's still costing you writes.

### Maintenance
- **`ANALYZE <table>`** after creating an index — refresh planner statistics so the new index is used immediately.
- **`REINDEX CONCURRENTLY`** if an index has bloated (long-running transactions, frequent updates) — rebuilds without blocking writes.
- **`pg_stat_user_indexes`** — monitor which indexes are used and which aren't.

### Common reasoning mistakes
- 'It has an index, why is it slow?' — the index might be too unselective for the planner to prefer.
- Three single-column indexes when a composite would be faster.
- Wrong column order in the composite (low-selectivity column first).
- Forgetting to `ANALYZE` after creating an index.
- Indexing every column 'just in case' (write cost adds up; unused indexes are pure cost).
- Adding indexes without reading `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` first to confirm the bottleneck.

### You just learned…
Composite indexes combine multiple columns into one B-tree. Column order matters: equality first in order of selectivity, range columns last. The leftmost prefix rule governs which queries can use the index. `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` is the cheapest tool for diagnosing index choice. `ANALYZE` after creating an index so the planner sees the new statistics immediately.
